1: <header>Life cycle assessment of digestate post-treatment and utilization </header>
Elisavet Angouria-Tsorochidou a,b , Michele Seghetta Anne Trémier , Marianne Thomsen a,b,*

<list>a Aarhus University, Department of Environmental Science, Research group on EcoIndustrial System Analysis, Frederiksborgvej 399, Postboks 358, DK-4000 Roskilde, Denmark

2:
<list>Digestate post-treatment can contribute to optimized circular resource management. 
Three digestate post-treatment scenarios are evaluated with life cycle assessment. 
Digestate post-treatment enhances the concentration of citrate soluble P. 
Transport of dried digestate is a preferred solution when local use is not feasible. 
Different quality of digestate products can answer diverse agronomic needs.</list>

4: Article history: Received 27 July 2021 Received in revised form 20 December 2021 Accepted 25 December 2021 Available online 3 January 2022
Digestate post-treatment Life cycle assessment Biofertilzer production Circular nutrient management
5: Three digestate utilization scenarios for bio-fertilizer production are evaluated with life cycle assessment. The aim is to determine the environmental performance of the digestate post-treatment with the goal to decrease the loss of nitrogen and phosphorus, support circular nutrient management, and increase the substitution of mineral fertilizers. The func- tional unit (FU) of the study is the utilization of 1 kg dry matter raw digestate, in three scenario designs. Scenario 1 (S1) describes a system where the raw digestate is directly spread on soil. In scenario 2 (S2) the raw digestate is processed by centrifugation with two recovered phases (liquid and solid digestate), which are spread on agricultural soil. In scenario 3 (S3) a more advanced post-treatment system is modelled, where the raw digestate is phase separated with centrifugation followed by drying of the solid digestate and further processing of the liquid digestate with a mem- brane filtration and a reverse osmosis unit. The studied scenarios show a global warming potential ranging from 0.14 (S3) to - 0.36 (S1) kg CO2 eq per FU. The fossil resource depletion per FU was decreased in scenario 1 ( - 0.053 kg oil eq) and scenario 2 -0.049 kg oil eq) but increased in scenario 3 (0.002 kg oil eq). The terrestrial acid- ification potential ranges from 0.09 (S3) to 0.18 (S1) kg SO2 per FU. The digestate post-treatment is a sustainable so- lution able to tackle the problem of excess nutrients and their management in agricultural areas. It could replace conventional nitrogen removal processes (aerobic biological treatment) by a valorization chain keeping the nutrients in closed loop.

Biomass valorization and bio-product optimization have received a lot of attention at the European and national levels (European Commission, 2012, 2018; The Danish Government, 2013). Considering the growing en- ergy and fertilizer needs (Eurostat, 2021), anaerobic digestion (AD) proved to be an efficient and cost-effective technology for organic waste treatment,

able to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from waste management activities (Niero et al., 2014; Rajendran and Murthy, 2019) and contribute to re- source recovery (Cheong et al., 2020) and circular economy (Wainaina et al., 2020). The process of AD generates biogas, which is used for bio- energy production, and digestate, the residue of the technology, which has fertilizing potential and can be applied on agricultural soil (Tampio et al., 2016).

The digestate quality depends on the origin of the organic waste and the post-treatment technology (Angouria-Tsorochidou and Thomsen, 2021). It contains organic matter, fertilizing elements, and potentially micro- pollutants, such as cadmium (Pizzol et al., 2014). To fully utilize the fertil- izing potential of the digestate, while avoiding the risk for soil contamina- tion, there is the need to develop innovative nutrient management strategies capable of isolating the nutrients intended for valorization while removing or immobilizing the unwanted compounds. This can be achieved by the smart use of organic feedstock (Wang et al., 2021), through diversification and optimization of the nutrient content in the solid and liq- uid fertilizer products.

Different technologies are developed to enable the handling and/or re- covery of the digestate (Lu and Xu, 2021) according to their water content (Flotats et al., 2009; Vaneeckhaute et al., 2017). Solid and low moisture digestate can be dried to facilitate its transport and its final use as a fertil- izer, with or without pelletizing, or can be composted to stabilize its organic matter while performing its hygienization. For liquid digestate, a mechani- cal separation of phases (screening, screw press, or centrifugation) is often the first step, which allows concentrating the suspended solids and some as- sociated elements in the solid fraction. The solid phase obtained after sepa- ration is treated as described above, while the liquid phase can undergo various post-treatment methods depending on the objective to either re- move or concentrate nutrients to produce a suitable fertilizer. Membrane separation is a technology that through physical barriers for selective nutri- ent capture can concentrate the desired substances in smaller volumes, achieving reduced costs of storage and transportation, while minimizing the losses of nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) compounds (Masse et al., 2007; Thörneby et al., 1999).

Technical and scientific knowledge on the treatment technologies is highly variable. Among them, phase separation has been widely character- ized in terms of separation efficiency and energy consumption, especially concerning manure (Béline et al., 2004; Burton and Turner, 2003; Daumer et al., 2003) and more recently about digestate from other types of feedstock (Guilayn et al., 2019). Areas with intensive livestock produc- tion often face excess of nutrients, especially N, sourced from animal ma- nure compared to the nutrient and fertilization needs of the available adjacent arable land and grassland soil. Post-treatment technologies can be incorporated to reduce the unsustainable excess of nutrients by increas- ing mineralization and by partitioning the N content between the liquid and solid fertilizer products. This partitioning lowers the N/P ratio in the solid fertilizer and results in higher fertilizer value due to the more balanced fer- tilization effect. However, by 2015 only 3% of the European digestate was upgraded to high-value products (Dahlin et al., 2015).

The evaluation of the environmental performance of these post- treatment technologies is important to ensure future optimized circular nu- trient management systems. One of the major environmental challenges of digestate utilization is the N losses through volatilization, which contrib- utes to air emissions (especially ammonia (NH3) and nitrous oxide (N20)) and result in losses of fertilizing value. Post-treatment and spreading of digestate products can generate different types of greenhouse gases, such as carbon dioxide (CO2), N20, and volatilized NH3 (Bertora et al., 2008) but certain post-treatment practices can also help reducing a share of these emissions compared to conventional practices without pre- treatment (Styles et al., 2018). For this reason, it is necessary to assess the sustainability of these post-treatment technologies regarding their effi- ciency in recycling of nutrients and associated environmental impacts.

In this study, a life cycle assessment (LCA) was performed to compare the environmental performance of two post-treatment technology designs and a baseline scenario corresponding to direct spreading of raw digestate.

The digestate post-treatment technology scenarios are simulated based on a French AD plant treating manure, which is collected from several farms and is co-digested with agro-food industry residues and sludge. The measure- ment of emissions related to digestate application on soil after post- treatment were performed with experimental setups. The LCA aims at exploring the environmental impacts of the potential conversion pathways processing digestate into high quality organic fertilizer products according to the European (European Commission, 2019) and national regulations (Danish Ministry of Environment, 2018; French Minintry of Agriculture and Food, 2013).

Post-treatment and spreading of digestate products can generate different types of greenhouse gases, such as carbon dioxide (CO2), N20, and volatilized NH3 (Bertora et al., 2008) but certain post-treatment practices can also help reducing a share of these emissions compared to conventional practices without pre- treatment (Styles et al., 2018). For this reason, it is necessary to assess the sustainability of these post-treatment technologies regarding their effi- ciency in recycling of nutrients and associated environmental impacts.

In this study, a life cycle assessment (LCA) was performed to compare the environmental performance of two post-treatment technology designs and a baseline scenario corresponding to direct spreading of raw digestate.

The digestate post-treatment technology scenarios are simulated based on a French AD plant treating manure, which is collected from several farms and is co-digested with agro-food industry residues and sludge. The measure- ment of emissions related to digestate application on soil after post- treatment were performed with experimental setups. The LCA aims at exploring the environmental impacts of the potential conversion pathways processing digestate into high quality organic fertilizer products according to the European (European Commission, 2019) and national regulations (Danish Ministry of Environment, 2018; French Minintry of Agriculture and Food, 2013).
12: <header>2. Materials and methods </header>

The LCA follows four main steps: i) goal and scope definition, ii) life cycle inventory (LCI), iii) life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) and iv) inter- pretation, performed in accordance with the international standards ISO 14040-44 (International Organization for Standardization, 2006).
2.1. Goal and scope definition

The goal of the study is to compare three designs for the management of digestate from a centralized AD plant. The study takes as point of departure a French case study with the aim to increase the efficiency in the recovery and reuse of nutrients from the raw digestate. The organic fertilizer can be used locally and/or by exporting the excess nutrients to regional and global markets, thereby closing the biological nutrient loop and increasing the European fertilizer self-supply.
The functional unit (FU) is defined as the "utilization of 1 kg dry matter (DM) raw digestate produced by a centralized AD plant".
2.2. Life cycle inventory

The study focuses on the utilization of digestate from a centralized mesophilic digester plant, with a retention time of 60 days and a post- digestion with a retention time of 14 days, treating mainly pig liquid ma- nure from several farms mixed with sewage sludge and waste from agro- food industries. The assessment covers all the steps of the raw digestate management and utilization, excluding its production. The system bound- ary includes the conversion steps (e.g., separation, drying, and membrane filtration) and spreading of the products on agricultural soil.
Three different utilization pathways of raw digestate are assessed. The system boundary is shown with dashed lines in Fig. 1.

Scenario 1 (S1) describes a system where the raw digestate is directly spread on local agricultural soil surrounding the AD plant, without post- treatment. In scenario 2 (S2), the raw digestate is processed by phase sepa- ration (centrifugation). Two phases (liquid and solid digestate) are ob- tained without any further processing. Both the liquid and the solid digestate are applied on local agricultural soils. Scenario 3 (S3) describes a more advanced post-treatment system. The raw digestate is phase sepa- rated by centrifugation followed by drying of the solid digestate and further processing of the liquid digestate with a membrane filtration unit. The first step of filtration for the liquid digestate consists of an ultra-filtration (UF) that produces two liquid fractions, the UF retentate and the UF filtrate. The UF retentate is applied on local agricultural soil. The UF filtrate un- dergoes a second filtration through a reverse osmosis (RO) unit. The RO concentrate is locally valorized as liquid fertilizer, while the filtrate, i.e., demineralized water, substitutes tap water used in the digestate pro- cessing. The chemical characteristics of the raw digestate, the solid and liq- uid fertilizer in S1, and the dried digestate, UF retentate, RO concentrate and demineralized water in S3, were measured using triplicate samples col- lected from the centralized plant. The detailed analytical methods and the quality of the digestate products are presented in Appendix A1.

The storage of digestate can contribute to the environmental impact with emissions, such as NH3 and CH4 (Styles et al., 2018), but could poten- tially reduce emissions during and after spreading. The emissions from stor- age were not directly measured by the experimental setup, but the

|Scenario. 1|Scenario. 1|Scenario. 1|Scenario. 1|Scenario. 1|Scenario. 1|Scenario. 1|Scenario. 1|Scenario. 1|Avoided products Anaerobic digestion|RD|RD|RD|RD|RD|Transport||Land application|Land application|Mineral fertilizer Anaerobic digestion|Scenacio 2|Scenacio 2|Scenacio 2|Scenacio 2|Scenacio 2|Scenacio 2|Scenacio 2|Scenacio 2|Scenacio 2| Anaerobic digestion|RD Centrifuge|||SD|SD|Transport||Land application||Mineral fertilizer ||||LD|LD|Transport||Land application|Land application|Mineral fertilizer |Scenario 3|Scenario 3|Scenario 3|Scenario 3|Scenario 3|Scenario 3|Scenario 3|Scenario 3|Scenario 3| Anaerobic digestion|Centrifuge|SD|Drying|Drying|Drying|Transport||Land application|Land application|Mineral fertilizer ||LL|Ultrafiltration -UFr|Ultrafiltration -UFr|Ultrafiltration -UFr|Transport||Land application|Land application|Mineral fertilizer |||UFf|||||Land application|Land application|Mineral fertilizer |||Reverse|osmosis|ROc|Transport||Land application|Land application|Mineral fertilizer ||Demineralised water|Demineralised water|Demineralised water|Demineralised water|Demineralised water|Demineralised water|Demineralised water|Demineralised water|Tap water

Fig. 1. Illustration of the assessed scenarios (RD: raw digestate, SD: solid digestate, LD: liquid digestate, UFr: Ultrafiltration retentate, UFf: ultrafiltration filtrate, ROc: reverse osmosis concentrate).

emissions after spreading were measured on non-stored digestate. The lat- ter were used as a proxy in this study. However, long-term storage of digestate can have significant contributions to the environmental impacts. The digestate storage and its associated environmental impacts are further discussed in Section 3.
The digestate is considered both to be valorized locally and to be exported to other markets, as described below:

<list>In S1, the raw digestate is transported for 10 km prior to spreading on ag- ricultural soils. 
In S2, both solid and liquid fractions are transported for 10 km prior to spreading on agricultural soils. 
In S3, the transport distance for the dried digestate is 200 km, while the UF retentate and RO concentrate are transported for 10 km prior to spreading on agricultural soils. The demineralized water is stored and used for dilution at the entry of centrifugation, therefore, substituting the consumption of local tap water.</list>

The solid digestate in S2 and the dried solid digestate in S3 are solid fer- tilizer products, while the raw digestate in S1, the liquid digestate in S2, the UF retentate and the RO concentrate in S3 are liquid fertilizers products. The change in the nutrient content of the digestate products in the different scenarios does not affect the transportation distance of the digestate but is considered as a factor in the spreading activities and the potential area COV- ered. The fuel consumption for transport is expressed in tkm.kg DM. A detailed inventory is presented in Appendix A2.

The French fertilization directive (JORF, 2011) limits the amount of N allowed to be spread on agricultural soil to 170 kg N.ha Phosphorus is not regulated by law in France, but local prescriptions are given for equili- brated fertilization. Regulations exist in other countries, such as Germany, Netherlands, and Denmark (Danish Environmental Protection Agency, 2017). In the latter, limits for P spreading range between 30 and 43 kg P.ha A limit of 30 kg P.ha has been chosen in this study, which results in an N/P ratio of 5.67. The spreading area covered by the digestate derived fertilizer products was calculated based on the N limit when the N/P ratio was above 5.67 and on the P limit when the N/P ratio was below 5.67 (see Eq. (1)).
or SA (1) N or P limit

where SA is the spreading area expressed in ha-kg DM, FCN or P is the N or P content in the fertilizers in kg P or N-kg DM, and N or P limit is the N or P fertilizer regulation of, respectively, 170 kg N.ha and 30 kg P.ha The spreading of the digestate is modelled to be performed with an ag- ricultural tractor and trailer. According to Lacour et al. (2012), the fuel

consumption (diesel) for spreading of solid products is 11.25 while for the liquid products it is 13 L:ha The impact of digestate spread- ing is calculated according to Eq. (2), in order to transform the fuel con- sumption into units of the agricultural trailer used (tkm), by considering the spreading area calculated in Eq. (1) and the type of digestate (solid and liquid). The impacts from digestate spreading include the machinery and the emissions associated with its operation. The results for the spread- ing of digestate are summarized in Appendix A2.
DS SA

where, DS is the impact from digestate spreading with an agricultural trac- tor, expressed in units of tkm-kg DM, FC is the fuel consumption in L.ha according to Lacour et al. (2012), SWDiesel is the specific weight of diesel, 0.832 kg-L 1 and FCdatabase is the fuel consumption provided in the Ecoinvent database, 0.0436 kg diesel-tkm 1 (Wernet et al., 2016).

The fertilizer products spread on the soil replace mineral N and P fertil- izer according to the fertilizing value of the output products from the post- treatment scenarios. The bioavailable P content of the output products that substitutes mineral fertilizer has been measured as the citrate soluble frac- tion. The latter substitutes phosphate (P2O5) mineral fertilizer. The avoided processes included the production of mineral phosphate fertilizer (Wernet et al., 2016), and the direct emissions of heavy metals (Gambus and Wieczorek, 2012) and phosphorus leaching to soil. The substitution factor for the N content of the output products was 40% of the total N (Moller et al., 2009) and the mineral fertilizer substituted was ammonium nitrate (NH4NO3). The avoided processes included the production of mineral am- monium nitrate fertilizer (Wernet et al., 2016), N2O emissions to air, NO3 and NH4 emissions to water adopted from Yoshida et al. (2016), and emissions of heavy metals (Gambus and Wieczorek, 2012) and N leaching to soil. The effect of the N and P substitution methodology is fur- ther assessed with a sensitivity analysis.

Laboratory measurements were performed to define the mineral and or- ganic content of N in the output products. The NH3 emissions after spread- ing for each digestate product were measured through laboratory experiments with wind tunnels. The NH3 at the entry and exit points of the tunnel was trapped in acid solution and measured, and the NH3 emitted was calculated as the ratio of NH3 trapped per unit of digestate product ap- plied for the experiment. The N2O emissions were also measured using a laboratory set up for three consecutive months after the digestate applica- tion. The modelling of the heavy metal emissions follows a conservative ap- proach with all the content to be considered as emissions to soil. Further information is provided in Appendix A3.

Details about the digestate-derived products and the quality of the digestate in each scenario are provided in Appendix A1. Data on energy consumption and transportation distances for the three scenarios are pro- vided in Appendix A2. Data on the nutrient flows and associated air and soil emissions from the treatment of the digestate and the methods for the laboratory emission measurements are provided in Appendix A3, along with the methods and experimental setup used for the laboratory measure- ments. Data on N/P ratio, the agricultural area covered (spreading area) and the amount of mineral fertilizer products substituted are provided in Appendix A4. A detailed list of the French standards for soil improvers and fertilizers are given in Appendix A5. The mass flows of the post- treatment scenarios are given in Appendix A6, and last, selected product quality measures and Danish and European regulatory standards are given in Appendix A7.

The NH3 emissions after spread- ing for each digestate product were measured through laboratory experiments with wind tunnels. The NH3 at the entry and exit points of the tunnel was trapped in acid solution and measured, and the NH3 emitted was calculated as the ratio of NH3 trapped per unit of digestate product ap- plied for the experiment. The N2O emissions were also measured using a laboratory set up for three consecutive months after the digestate applica- tion. The modelling of the heavy metal emissions follows a conservative ap- proach with all the content to be considered as emissions to soil. Further information is provided in Appendix A3.

Details about the digestate-derived products and the quality of the digestate in each scenario are provided in Appendix A1. Data on energy consumption and transportation distances for the three scenarios are pro- vided in Appendix A2. Data on the nutrient flows and associated air and soil emissions from the treatment of the digestate and the methods for the laboratory emission measurements are provided in Appendix A3, along with the methods and experimental setup used for the laboratory measure- ments. Data on N/P ratio, the agricultural area covered (spreading area) and the amount of mineral fertilizer products substituted are provided in Appendix A4. A detailed list of the French standards for soil improvers and fertilizers are given in Appendix A5. The mass flows of the post- treatment scenarios are given in Appendix A6, and last, selected product quality measures and Danish and European regulatory standards are given in Appendix A7.
15: <header>2. Materials and methods </header>

The LCA follows four main steps: i) goal and scope definition, ii) life cycle inventory (LCI), iii) life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) and iv) inter- pretation, performed in accordance with the international standards ISO 14040-44 (International Organization for Standardization, 2006).
2.1. Goal and scope definition

The goal of the study is to compare three designs for the management of digestate from a centralized AD plant. The study takes as point of departure a French case study with the aim to increase the efficiency in the recovery and reuse of nutrients from the raw digestate. The organic fertilizer can be used locally and/or by exporting the excess nutrients to regional and global markets, thereby closing the biological nutrient loop and increasing the European fertilizer self-supply.
The functional unit (FU) is defined as the "utilization of 1 kg dry matter (DM) raw digestate produced by a centralized AD plant".
2.2. Life cycle inventory

The study focuses on the utilization of digestate from a centralized mesophilic digester plant, with a retention time of 60 days and a post- digestion with a retention time of 14 days, treating mainly pig liquid ma- nure from several farms mixed with sewage sludge and waste from agro- food industries. The assessment covers all the steps of the raw digestate management and utilization, excluding its production. The system bound- ary includes the conversion steps (e.g., separation, drying, and membrane filtration) and spreading of the products on agricultural soil.
Three different utilization pathways of raw digestate are assessed. The system boundary is shown with dashed lines in Fig. 1.

Scenario 1 (S1) describes a system where the raw digestate is directly spread on local agricultural soil surrounding the AD plant, without post- treatment. In scenario 2 (S2), the raw digestate is processed by phase sepa- ration (centrifugation). Two phases (liquid and solid digestate) are ob- tained without any further processing. Both the liquid and the solid digestate are applied on local agricultural soils. Scenario 3 (S3) describes a more advanced post-treatment system. The raw digestate is phase sepa- rated by centrifugation followed by drying of the solid digestate and further processing of the liquid digestate with a membrane filtration unit. The first step of filtration for the liquid digestate consists of an ultra-filtration (UF) that produces two liquid fractions, the UF retentate and the UF filtrate. The UF retentate is applied on local agricultural soil. The UF filtrate un- dergoes a second filtration through a reverse osmosis (RO) unit. The RO concentrate is locally valorized as liquid fertilizer, while the filtrate, i.e., demineralized water, substitutes tap water used in the digestate pro- cessing. The chemical characteristics of the raw digestate, the solid and liq- uid fertilizer in S1, and the dried digestate, UF retentate, RO concentrate and demineralized water in S3, were measured using triplicate samples col- lected from the centralized plant. The detailed analytical methods and the quality of the digestate products are presented in Appendix A1.

The storage of digestate can contribute to the environmental impact with emissions, such as NH3 and CH4 (Styles et al., 2018), but could poten- tially reduce emissions during and after spreading. The emissions from stor- age were not directly measured by the experimental setup, but the

emissions after spreading were measured on non-stored digestate. The lat- ter were used as a proxy in this study. However, long-term storage of digestate can have significant contributions to the environmental impacts. The digestate storage and its associated environmental impacts are further discussed in Section 3.
The digestate is considered both to be valorized locally and to be exported to other markets, as described below:

<list>In S1, the raw digestate is transported for 10 km prior to spreading on ag- ricultural soils. 
In S2, both solid and liquid fractions are transported for 10 km prior to spreading on agricultural soils. 
In S3, the transport distance for the dried digestate is 200 km, while the UF retentate and RO concentrate are transported for 10 km prior to spreading on agricultural soils. The demineralized water is stored and used for dilution at the entry of centrifugation, therefore, substituting the consumption of local tap water.</list>

The solid digestate in S2 and the dried solid digestate in S3 are solid fer- tilizer products, while the raw digestate in S1, the liquid digestate in S2, the UF retentate and the RO concentrate in S3 are liquid fertilizers products. The change in the nutrient content of the digestate products in the different scenarios does not affect the transportation distance of the digestate but is considered as a factor in the spreading activities and the potential area COV- ered. The fuel consumption for transport is expressed in tkm.kg DM. A detailed inventory is presented in Appendix A2.

The French fertilization directive (JORF, 2011) limits the amount of N allowed to be spread on agricultural soil to 170 kg N.ha Phosphorus is not regulated by law in France, but local prescriptions are given for equili- brated fertilization. Regulations exist in other countries, such as Germany, Netherlands, and Denmark (Danish Environmental Protection Agency, 2017). In the latter, limits for P spreading range between 30 and 43 kg P.ha A limit of 30 kg P.ha has been chosen in this study, which results in an N/P ratio of 5.67. The spreading area covered by the digestate derived fertilizer products was calculated based on the N limit when the N/P ratio was above 5.67 and on the P limit when the N/P ratio was below 5.67 (see Eq. (1)).

According to Lacour et al. (2012), the fuel

consumption (diesel) for spreading of solid products is 11.25 while for the liquid products it is 13 L:ha The impact of digestate spread- ing is calculated according to Eq. (2), in order to transform the fuel con- sumption into units of the agricultural trailer used (tkm), by considering the spreading area calculated in Eq. (1) and the type of digestate (solid and liquid). The impacts from digestate spreading include the machinery and the emissions associated with its operation. The results for the spread- ing of digestate are summarized in Appendix A2.

where, DS is the impact from digestate spreading with an agricultural trac- tor, expressed in units of tkm-kg DM, FC is the fuel consumption in L.ha according to Lacour et al. (2012), SWDiesel is the specific weight of diesel, 0.832 kg-L 1 and FCdatabase is the fuel consumption provided in the Ecoinvent database, 0.0436 kg diesel-tkm 1 (Wernet et al., 2016).

The fertilizer products spread on the soil replace mineral N and P fertil- izer according to the fertilizing value of the output products from the post- treatment scenarios. The bioavailable P content of the output products that substitutes mineral fertilizer has been measured as the citrate soluble frac- tion. The latter substitutes phosphate (P2O5) mineral fertilizer. The avoided processes included the production of mineral phosphate fertilizer (Wernet et al., 2016), and the direct emissions of heavy metals (Gambus and Wieczorek, 2012) and phosphorus leaching to soil. The substitution factor for the N content of the output products was 40% of the total N (Moller et al., 2009) and the mineral fertilizer substituted was ammonium nitrate (NH4NO3). The avoided processes included the production of mineral am- monium nitrate fertilizer (Wernet et al., 2016), N2O emissions to air, NO3 and NH4 emissions to water adopted from Yoshida et al. (2016), and emissions of heavy metals (Gambus and Wieczorek, 2012) and N leaching to soil. The effect of the N and P substitution methodology is fur- ther assessed with a sensitivity analysis.

Laboratory measurements were performed to define the mineral and or- ganic content of N in the output products. The NH3 emissions after spread- ing for each digestate product were measured through laboratory experiments with wind tunnels. The NH3 at the entry and exit points of the tunnel was trapped in acid solution and measured, and the NH3 emitted was calculated as the ratio of NH3 trapped per unit of digestate product ap- plied for the experiment. The N2O emissions were also measured using a laboratory set up for three consecutive months after the digestate applica- tion. The modelling of the heavy metal emissions follows a conservative ap- proach with all the content to be considered as emissions to soil. Further information is provided in Appendix A3.

Details about the digestate-derived products and the quality of the digestate in each scenario are provided in Appendix A1. Data on energy consumption and transportation distances for the three scenarios are pro- vided in Appendix A2. Data on the nutrient flows and associated air and soil emissions from the treatment of the digestate and the methods for the laboratory emission measurements are provided in Appendix A3, along with the methods and experimental setup used for the laboratory measure- ments. Data on N/P ratio, the agricultural area covered (spreading area) and the amount of mineral fertilizer products substituted are provided in Appendix A4. A detailed list of the French standards for soil improvers and fertilizers are given in Appendix A5. The mass flows of the post- treatment scenarios are given in Appendix A6, and last, selected product quality measures and Danish and European regulatory standards are given in Appendix A7.

(2016), and emissions of heavy metals (Gambus and Wieczorek, 2012) and N leaching to soil. The effect of the N and P substitution methodology is fur- ther assessed with a sensitivity analysis.

Laboratory measurements were performed to define the mineral and or- ganic content of N in the output products. The NH3 emissions after spread- ing for each digestate product were measured through laboratory experiments with wind tunnels. The NH3 at the entry and exit points of the tunnel was trapped in acid solution and measured, and the NH3 emitted was calculated as the ratio of NH3 trapped per unit of digestate product ap- plied for the experiment. The N2O emissions were also measured using a laboratory set up for three consecutive months after the digestate applica- tion. The modelling of the heavy metal emissions follows a conservative ap- proach with all the content to be considered as emissions to soil. Further information is provided in Appendix A3.

Details about the digestate-derived products and the quality of the digestate in each scenario are provided in Appendix A1. Data on energy consumption and transportation distances for the three scenarios are pro- vided in Appendix A2. Data on the nutrient flows and associated air and soil emissions from the treatment of the digestate and the methods for the laboratory emission measurements are provided in Appendix A3, along with the methods and experimental setup used for the laboratory measure- ments. Data on N/P ratio, the agricultural area covered (spreading area) and the amount of mineral fertilizer products substituted are provided in Appendix A4. A detailed list of the French standards for soil improvers and fertilizers are given in Appendix A5. The mass flows of the post- treatment scenarios are given in Appendix A6, and last, selected product quality measures and Danish and European regulatory standards are given in Appendix A7.
18: <header>2. Materials and methods </header>

The LCA follows four main steps: i) goal and scope definition, ii) life cycle inventory (LCI), iii) life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) and iv) inter- pretation, performed in accordance with the international standards ISO 14040-44 (International Organization for Standardization, 2006).
2.1. Goal and scope definition

The goal of the study is to compare three designs for the management of digestate from a centralized AD plant. The study takes as point of departure a French case study with the aim to increase the efficiency in the recovery and reuse of nutrients from the raw digestate. The organic fertilizer can be used locally and/or by exporting the excess nutrients to regional and global markets, thereby closing the biological nutrient loop and increasing the European fertilizer self-supply.
The functional unit (FU) is defined as the "utilization of 1 kg dry matter (DM) raw digestate produced by a centralized AD plant".
2.2. Life cycle inventory

The study focuses on the utilization of digestate from a centralized mesophilic digester plant, with a retention time of 60 days and a post- digestion with a retention time of 14 days, treating mainly pig liquid ma- nure from several farms mixed with sewage sludge and waste from agro- food industries. The assessment covers all the steps of the raw digestate management and utilization, excluding its production. The system bound- ary includes the conversion steps (e.g., separation, drying, and membrane filtration) and spreading of the products on agricultural soil.
Three different utilization pathways of raw digestate are assessed. The system boundary is shown with dashed lines in Fig. 1.

Scenario 1 (S1) describes a system where the raw digestate is directly spread on local agricultural soil surrounding the AD plant, without post- treatment. In scenario 2 (S2), the raw digestate is processed by phase sepa- ration (centrifugation). Two phases (liquid and solid digestate) are ob- tained without any further processing. Both the liquid and the solid digestate are applied on local agricultural soils. Scenario 3 (S3) describes a more advanced post-treatment system. The raw digestate is phase sepa- rated by centrifugation followed by drying of the solid digestate and further processing of the liquid digestate with a membrane filtration unit. The first step of filtration for the liquid digestate consists of an ultra-filtration (UF) that produces two liquid fractions, the UF retentate and the UF filtrate. The UF retentate is applied on local agricultural soil. The UF filtrate un- dergoes a second filtration through a reverse osmosis (RO) unit. The RO concentrate is locally valorized as liquid fertilizer, while the filtrate, i.e., demineralized water, substitutes tap water used in the digestate pro- cessing. The chemical characteristics of the raw digestate, the solid and liq- uid fertilizer in S1, and the dried digestate, UF retentate, RO concentrate and demineralized water in S3, were measured using triplicate samples col- lected from the centralized plant. The detailed analytical methods and the quality of the digestate products are presented in Appendix A1.

The storage of digestate can contribute to the environmental impact with emissions, such as NH3 and CH4 (Styles et al., 2018), but could poten- tially reduce emissions during and after spreading. The emissions from stor- age were not directly measured by the experimental setup, but the

emissions after spreading were measured on non-stored digestate. The lat- ter were used as a proxy in this study. However, long-term storage of digestate can have significant contributions to the environmental impacts. The digestate storage and its associated environmental impacts are further discussed in Section 3.
The digestate is considered both to be valorized locally and to be exported to other markets, as described below:

<list>In S1, the raw digestate is transported for 10 km prior to spreading on ag- ricultural soils. 
In S2, both solid and liquid fractions are transported for 10 km prior to spreading on agricultural soils. 
In S3, the transport distance for the dried digestate is 200 km, while the UF retentate and RO concentrate are transported for 10 km prior to spreading on agricultural soils. The demineralized water is stored and used for dilution at the entry of centrifugation, therefore, substituting the consumption of local tap water.</list>

The solid digestate in S2 and the dried solid digestate in S3 are solid fer- tilizer products, while the raw digestate in S1, the liquid digestate in S2, the UF retentate and the RO concentrate in S3 are liquid fertilizers products. The change in the nutrient content of the digestate products in the different scenarios does not affect the transportation distance of the digestate but is considered as a factor in the spreading activities and the potential area COV- ered. The fuel consumption for transport is expressed in tkm.kg DM. A detailed inventory is presented in Appendix A2.

The French fertilization directive (JORF, 2011) limits the amount of N allowed to be spread on agricultural soil to 170 kg N.ha Phosphorus is not regulated by law in France, but local prescriptions are given for equili- brated fertilization. Regulations exist in other countries, such as Germany, Netherlands, and Denmark (Danish Environmental Protection Agency, 2017). In the latter, limits for P spreading range between 30 and 43 kg P.ha A limit of 30 kg P.ha has been chosen in this study, which results in an N/P ratio of 5.67. The spreading area covered by the digestate derived fertilizer products was calculated based on the N limit when the N/P ratio was above 5.67 and on the P limit when the N/P ratio was below 5.67 (see Eq. (1)).

According to Lacour et al. (2012), the fuel

The results for the spread- ing of digestate are summarized in Appendix A2.

The fertilizer products spread on the soil replace mineral N and P fertil- izer according to the fertilizing value of the output products from the post- treatment scenarios. The bioavailable P content of the output products that substitutes mineral fertilizer has been measured as the citrate soluble frac- tion. The latter substitutes phosphate (P2O5) mineral fertilizer. (2016), and emissions of heavy metals (Gambus and Wieczorek, 2012) and N leaching to soil. The effect of the N and P substitution methodology is fur- ther assessed with a sensitivity analysis.

The NH3 at the entry and exit points of the tunnel was trapped in acid solution and measured, and the NH3 emitted was calculated as the ratio of NH3 trapped per unit of digestate product ap- plied for the experiment. The N2O emissions were also measured using a laboratory set up for three consecutive months after the digestate applica- tion. The modelling of the heavy metal emissions follows a conservative ap- proach with all the content to be considered as emissions to soil. Further information is provided in Appendix A3.

Details about the digestate-derived products and the quality of the digestate in each scenario are provided in Appendix A1. Data on energy consumption and transportation distances for the three scenarios are pro- vided in Appendix A2. Data on the nutrient flows and associated air and soil emissions from the treatment of the digestate and the methods for the laboratory emission measurements are provided in Appendix A3, along with the methods and experimental setup used for the laboratory measure- ments. Data on N/P ratio, the agricultural area covered (spreading area) and the amount of mineral fertilizer products substituted are provided in Appendix A4. A detailed list of the French standards for soil improvers and fertilizers are given in Appendix A5. The mass flows of the post- treatment scenarios are given in Appendix A6, and last, selected product quality measures and Danish and European regulatory standards are given in Appendix A7.

The substitution factor for the N content of the output products was 40% of the total N (Moller et al., 2009) and the mineral fertilizer substituted was ammonium nitrate (NH4NO3). The avoided processes included the production of mineral am- monium nitrate fertilizer (Wernet et al., 2016), N2O emissions to air, NO3 and NH4 emissions to water adopted from Yoshida et al. (2016), and emissions of heavy metals (Gambus and Wieczorek, 2012) and N leaching to soil. The effect of the N and P substitution methodology is fur- ther assessed with a sensitivity analysis.

Laboratory measurements were performed to define the mineral and or- ganic content of N in the output products. The NH3 emissions after spread- ing for each digestate product were measured through laboratory experiments with wind tunnels. The NH3 at the entry and exit points of the tunnel was trapped in acid solution and measured, and the NH3 emitted was calculated as the ratio of NH3 trapped per unit of digestate product ap- plied for the experiment. The N2O emissions were also measured using a laboratory set up for three consecutive months after the digestate applica- tion. The modelling of the heavy metal emissions follows a conservative ap- proach with all the content to be considered as emissions to soil. Further information is provided in Appendix A3.

Details about the digestate-derived products and the quality of the digestate in each scenario are provided in Appendix A1. Data on energy consumption and transportation distances for the three scenarios are pro- vided in Appendix A2. Data on the nutrient flows and associated air and soil emissions from the treatment of the digestate and the methods for the laboratory emission measurements are provided in Appendix A3, along with the methods and experimental setup used for the laboratory measure- ments. Data on N/P ratio, the agricultural area covered (spreading area) and the amount of mineral fertilizer products substituted are provided in Appendix A4. A detailed list of the French standards for soil improvers and fertilizers are given in Appendix A5. The mass flows of the post- treatment scenarios are given in Appendix A6, and last, selected product quality measures and Danish and European regulatory standards are given in Appendix A7.
21: <header>2. Materials and methods </header>

The LCA follows four main steps: i) goal and scope definition, ii) life cycle inventory (LCI), iii) life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) and iv) inter- pretation, performed in accordance with the international standards ISO 14040-44 (International Organization for Standardization, 2006).
2.1. Goal and scope definition

The goal of the study is to compare three designs for the management of digestate from a centralized AD plant. The study takes as point of departure a French case study with the aim to increase the efficiency in the recovery and reuse of nutrients from the raw digestate. The organic fertilizer can be used locally and/or by exporting the excess nutrients to regional and global markets, thereby closing the biological nutrient loop and increasing the European fertilizer self-supply.
The functional unit (FU) is defined as the "utilization of 1 kg dry matter (DM) raw digestate produced by a centralized AD plant".
2.2. Life cycle inventory

The study focuses on the utilization of digestate from a centralized mesophilic digester plant, with a retention time of 60 days and a post- digestion with a retention time of 14 days, treating mainly pig liquid ma- nure from several farms mixed with sewage sludge and waste from agro- food industries. The assessment covers all the steps of the raw digestate management and utilization, excluding its production. The system bound- ary includes the conversion steps (e.g., separation, drying, and membrane filtration) and spreading of the products on agricultural soil.
Three different utilization pathways of raw digestate are assessed. The system boundary is shown with dashed lines in Fig. 1.

Scenario 1 (S1) describes a system where the raw digestate is directly spread on local agricultural soil surrounding the AD plant, without post- treatment. In scenario 2 (S2), the raw digestate is processed by phase sepa- ration (centrifugation). Two phases (liquid and solid digestate) are ob- tained without any further processing. Both the liquid and the solid digestate are applied on local agricultural soils. Scenario 3 (S3) describes a more advanced post-treatment system. The raw digestate is phase sepa- rated by centrifugation followed by drying of the solid digestate and further processing of the liquid digestate with a membrane filtration unit. The first step of filtration for the liquid digestate consists of an ultra-filtration (UF) that produces two liquid fractions, the UF retentate and the UF filtrate. The UF retentate is applied on local agricultural soil. The UF filtrate un- dergoes a second filtration through a reverse osmosis (RO) unit. The RO concentrate is locally valorized as liquid fertilizer, while the filtrate, i.e., demineralized water, substitutes tap water used in the digestate pro- cessing. The chemical characteristics of the raw digestate, the solid and liq- uid fertilizer in S1, and the dried digestate, UF retentate, RO concentrate and demineralized water in S3, were measured using triplicate samples col- lected from the centralized plant. The detailed analytical methods and the quality of the digestate products are presented in Appendix A1.

The storage of digestate can contribute to the environmental impact with emissions, such as NH3 and CH4 (Styles et al., 2018), but could poten- tially reduce emissions during and after spreading. The emissions from stor- age were not directly measured by the experimental setup, but the

emissions after spreading were measured on non-stored digestate. The lat- ter were used as a proxy in this study. However, long-term storage of digestate can have significant contributions to the environmental impacts. The digestate storage and its associated environmental impacts are further discussed in Section 3.
The digestate is considered both to be valorized locally and to be exported to other markets, as described below:

<list>In S1, the raw digestate is transported for 10 km prior to spreading on ag- ricultural soils. 
In S2, both solid and liquid fractions are transported for 10 km prior to spreading on agricultural soils. 
In S3, the transport distance for the dried digestate is 200 km, while the UF retentate and RO concentrate are transported for 10 km prior to spreading on agricultural soils. The demineralized water is stored and used for dilution at the entry of centrifugation, therefore, substituting the consumption of local tap water.</list>

The solid digestate in S2 and the dried solid digestate in S3 are solid fer- tilizer products, while the raw digestate in S1, the liquid digestate in S2, the UF retentate and the RO concentrate in S3 are liquid fertilizers products. The change in the nutrient content of the digestate products in the different scenarios does not affect the transportation distance of the digestate but is considered as a factor in the spreading activities and the potential area COV- ered. The fuel consumption for transport is expressed in tkm.kg DM. A detailed inventory is presented in Appendix A2.

The French fertilization directive (JORF, 2011) limits the amount of N allowed to be spread on agricultural soil to 170 kg N.ha Phosphorus is not regulated by law in France, but local prescriptions are given for equili- brated fertilization. Regulations exist in other countries, such as Germany, Netherlands, and Denmark (Danish Environmental Protection Agency, 2017). In the latter, limits for P spreading range between 30 and 43 kg P.ha A limit of 30 kg P.ha has been chosen in this study, which results in an N/P ratio of 5.67. The spreading area covered by the digestate derived fertilizer products was calculated based on the N limit when the N/P ratio was above 5.67 and on the P limit when the N/P ratio was below 5.67 (see Eq. (1)).

Data on N/P ratio, the agricultural area covered (spreading area) and the amount of mineral fertilizer products substituted are provided in Appendix A4. The bioavailable P content of the output products that substitutes mineral fertilizer has been measured as the citrate soluble frac- tion. The latter substitutes phosphate (P2O5) mineral fertilizer. The avoided processes included the production of mineral phosphate fertilizer (Wernet et al., 2016), and the direct emissions of heavy metals (Gambus and Wieczorek, 2012) and phosphorus leaching to soil. The substitution factor for the N content of the output products was 40% of the total N (Moller et al., 2009) and the mineral fertilizer substituted was ammonium nitrate (NH4NO3). The avoided processes included the production of mineral am- monium nitrate fertilizer (Wernet et al., 2016), N2O emissions to air, NO3 and NH4 emissions to water adopted from Yoshida et al. (2016), and emissions of heavy metals (Gambus and Wieczorek, 2012) and N leaching to soil. The effect of the N and P substitution methodology is fur- ther assessed with a sensitivity analysis.

Laboratory measurements were performed to define the mineral and or- ganic content of N in the output products. The NH3 emissions after spread- ing for each digestate product were measured through laboratory experiments with wind tunnels. The NH3 at the entry and exit points of the tunnel was trapped in acid solution and measured, and the NH3 emitted was calculated as the ratio of NH3 trapped per unit of digestate product ap- plied for the experiment. The N2O emissions were also measured using a laboratory set up for three consecutive months after the digestate applica- tion. The modelling of the heavy metal emissions follows a conservative ap- proach with all the content to be considered as emissions to soil. Further information is provided in Appendix A3.

Details about the digestate-derived products and the quality of the digestate in each scenario are provided in Appendix A1. Data on energy consumption and transportation distances for the three scenarios are pro- vided in Appendix A2. Data on the nutrient flows and associated air and soil emissions from the treatment of the digestate and the methods for the laboratory emission measurements are provided in Appendix A3, along with the methods and experimental setup used for the laboratory measure- ments. Data on N/P ratio, the agricultural area covered (spreading area) and the amount of mineral fertilizer products substituted are provided in Appendix A4. A detailed list of the French standards for soil improvers and fertilizers are given in Appendix A5. The mass flows of the post- treatment scenarios are given in Appendix A6, and last, selected product quality measures and Danish and European regulatory standards are given in Appendix A7.
24: <header>2. Materials and methods </header>

The LCA follows four main steps: i) goal and scope definition, ii) life cycle inventory (LCI), iii) life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) and iv) inter- pretation, performed in accordance with the international standards ISO 14040-44 (International Organization for Standardization, 2006).
2.1. Goal and scope definition

The goal of the study is to compare three designs for the management of digestate from a centralized AD plant. The study takes as point of departure a French case study with the aim to increase the efficiency in the recovery and reuse of nutrients from the raw digestate. The organic fertilizer can be used locally and/or by exporting the excess nutrients to regional and global markets, thereby closing the biological nutrient loop and increasing the European fertilizer self-supply.
The functional unit (FU) is defined as the "utilization of 1 kg dry matter (DM) raw digestate produced by a centralized AD plant".
2.2. Life cycle inventory

The study focuses on the utilization of digestate from a centralized mesophilic digester plant, with a retention time of 60 days and a post- digestion with a retention time of 14 days, treating mainly pig liquid ma- nure from several farms mixed with sewage sludge and waste from agro- food industries. The assessment covers all the steps of the raw digestate management and utilization, excluding its production. The system bound- ary includes the conversion steps (e.g., separation, drying, and membrane filtration) and spreading of the products on agricultural soil.
Three different utilization pathways of raw digestate are assessed. The system boundary is shown with dashed lines in Fig. 1.

Scenario 1 (S1) describes a system where the raw digestate is directly spread on local agricultural soil surrounding the AD plant, without post- treatment. In scenario 2 (S2), the raw digestate is processed by phase sepa- ration (centrifugation). Two phases (liquid and solid digestate) are ob- tained without any further processing. Both the liquid and the solid digestate are applied on local agricultural soils. Scenario 3 (S3) describes a more advanced post-treatment system. The raw digestate is phase sepa- rated by centrifugation followed by drying of the solid digestate and further processing of the liquid digestate with a membrane filtration unit. The first step of filtration for the liquid digestate consists of an ultra-filtration (UF) that produces two liquid fractions, the UF retentate and the UF filtrate. The UF retentate is applied on local agricultural soil. The UF filtrate un- dergoes a second filtration through a reverse osmosis (RO) unit. The RO concentrate is locally valorized as liquid fertilizer, while the filtrate, i.e., demineralized water, substitutes tap water used in the digestate pro- cessing. The chemical characteristics of the raw digestate, the solid and liq- uid fertilizer in S1, and the dried digestate, UF retentate, RO concentrate and demineralized water in S3, were measured using triplicate samples col- lected from the centralized plant. The detailed analytical methods and the quality of the digestate products are presented in Appendix A1.

The storage of digestate can contribute to the environmental impact with emissions, such as NH3 and CH4 (Styles et al., 2018), but could poten- tially reduce emissions during and after spreading. The emissions from stor- age were not directly measured by the experimental setup, but the

However, long-term storage of digestate can have significant contributions to the environmental impacts. The digestate storage and its associated environmental impacts are further discussed in Section 3.
The digestate is considered both to be valorized locally and to be exported to other markets, as described below:

<list>In S1, the raw digestate is transported for 10 km prior to spreading on ag- ricultural soils. 
In S2, both solid and liquid fractions are transported for 10 km prior to spreading on agricultural soils. 
In S3, the transport distance for the dried digestate is 200 km, while the UF retentate and RO concentrate are transported for 10 km prior to spreading on agricultural soils. The demineralized water is stored and used for dilution at the entry of centrifugation, therefore, substituting the consumption of local tap water.</list>

The solid digestate in S2 and the dried solid digestate in S3 are solid fer- tilizer products, while the raw digestate in S1, the liquid digestate in S2, the UF retentate and the RO concentrate in S3 are liquid fertilizers products. The change in the nutrient content of the digestate products in the different scenarios does not affect the transportation distance of the digestate but is considered as a factor in the spreading activities and the potential area COV- ered. The fuel consumption for transport is expressed in tkm.kg DM. A detailed inventory is presented in Appendix A2.

The French fertilization directive (JORF, 2011) limits the amount of N allowed to be spread on agricultural soil to 170 kg N.ha Phosphorus is not regulated by law in France, but local prescriptions are given for equili- brated fertilization. Regulations exist in other countries, such as Germany, Netherlands, and Denmark (Danish Environmental Protection Agency, 2017). In the latter, limits for P spreading range between 30 and 43 kg P.ha A limit of 30 kg P.ha has been chosen in this study, which results in an N/P ratio of 5.67. The spreading area covered by the digestate derived fertilizer products was calculated based on the N limit when the N/P ratio was above 5.67 and on the P limit when the N/P ratio was below 5.67 (see Eq. 25: The LCIA was performed using the ReCiPe method (Huijbregts et al., 2017) at midpoint level. The impact assessment was performed in SimaPro software (7.3.3 version) (PRé Sustainability, 2021). The main emissions from the treatment are CO2, N20, CH4, and NH3 emissions to air after the spreading of the digestate, contributing to global warming and terrestrial acidification. As N leaching to soil was considered the mineral N content, thus, contributing to marine eutrophication potential through the charac- terization factors applied by the impact assessment methodology (1 kg N to agricultural soil = 0.13 kg N eq (Wernet et al., 2016)). A sensitivity anal- ysis was performed to evaluate the effect on the environmental perfor- mance when the total content of N was considered as emission to soil. The impact assessment methodology implicitly models N leaching to soil as a potent emission to marine eutrophication and P leaching to soil as a po- tent emission to freshwater eutrophication. Emissions to air from the en- ergy consumption during post-treatment and transport are associated with the impact categories climate change, fossil resource depletion, and acidification. Moreover, like mineral fertilizer products contain cadmium as an impurity, digestate can contain heavy metals and organic micro- pollutants transferred to the soil through spreading. The substitution of mineral fertilizer through the production and utilization of organic fertil- izers can reduce mineral resource scarcity. Thus, eight impact categories quantifying the resource use, human health, and environmental impacts were selected to assess the performance of the post-treatment designs. The selected impact categories for this study are:

<list>Climate change; in kg CO2 equivalents, [kg CO2 eq] 
Fossil resource depletion; in kg oil equivalents, [kg oil eq] 
Mineral resource scarcity, in kg copper equivalents [kg Cu eq] 
Human toxicity, non-carcinogenic; in 1,4-dichlorobenzene equivalents, [kg 1,4-DCB eq] 
Terrestrial ecotoxicity; in kg 1,4-dichlorobenzene equivalents, [kg 1,4- DCB eq] 
Freshwater eutrophication; in kg P equivalents, [kg P eq] 
Marine eutrophication; in kg N equivalents, [kg N eq] 
Terrestrial Acidification; in kg sulphur dioxide equivalents, [kg SO2 eq]</list>

27: A sensitivity analysis was performed to evaluate the effect of the substi- tution methodology followed in the study. In the scenarios modelled, only a fraction of P, the citrate soluble, and N, 40% of the total N, substitute min- eral fertilizers. This can have a strong effect on the calculated avoided im- pacts. In the sensitivity analysis, N and P are modelled to substitute mineral fertilizers according to their total content.

As N emission to soil was considered only the mineral N, in the main scenarios. This decision was taken to evaluate the N emissions on the same basis as the N substitution rates. In the sensitivity analysis, the total amount of N (mineral + organic) was considered as emission to soil, ac- counting for the total marine eutrophication potential caused by the
spreading of the different types of digestate. A similar approach was followed for P, and the freshwater eutrophication potential in the sensitiv- ity analysis.

A fourth scenario (S4) was modelled to evaluate the influence of trans- port versus stationary energy on the environmental performance. A com- parison between S3, in which the solid fraction is dried, and S4, in which the solid fraction is transported 200 km from the AD-plant (versus 10 km in S2) was performed.

29: The results from performing the LCIA on the three scenarios, using the methodology ReCiPe at midpoint (H), are shown in Fig. 2. The bars visual- lizing positive values represent environmental burdens while negative values represent avoided impacts and potential environmental benefits. The impacts presented in Fig. 2 result from 18 individual processes and are aggregated in three emission categories, i.e., direct, indirect, and avoided emissions, which are presented in Table 1. The direct emissions are defined as process emissions to soil and air from the biomass manage- ment. These are the emissions from the application of the raw (S1), solid (S2), liquid (S2), and dried (S3) digestate, and the UF retentate (S3), RO concentrate (S3), and the demineralized water (S3). An additional direct emission is the emission during post-treatment, which refers to the loss of resources during the treatment processes, only measured for S3. The indi- rect emissions include emission associated to all inputs necessary to operate the system. These are emissions from 8 processes, i.e., electricity for centri- fugation, for drying, and for ultrafiltration, the production of sulfuric acid, the production of polymer, dilution water, transport, and spreading activi- ties. The avoided emissions are related to the production and use of the substituted mineral fertilizer and the avoided consumption of tap water.

The net results show that S1 performs better at the impact categories of human non-carcinogenic toxicity, terrestrial ecotoxicity, and fossil resource scarcity, S2 performs better at the impact categories of global warming, freshwater eutrophication, and mineral resource scarcity, and S3 performs better at the impact categories of terrestrial acidification and marine eutrophication.

Direct emissions, or process emissions, reflect the environmental impacts contained in the sphere of influence of the system while indirect emissions reflect the energy and material intensity of the system originating from the background system. The direct emissions are responsible for the greater part of total impacts for all impact categories, except for the impact categories of fossil resource depletion, mineral resource scarcity, and terrestrial ecotoxicity. The fossil and mineral resource depletion impact categories have zero contribution to the direct emissions since all the anthropogenic fossil and mineral products are included in the indirect emis- sions. Mitigation of environmental impacts is due to the avoided emissions from mining activities performed by the mineral fertilizer industry, since there is a decreased market demand corresponding to the amount of min- eral fertilizers substituted by the organic fertilizers.

34: Emissions related to each scenario (IC: impact category, S: scenario, GW: global warming, FRS: fossil resource scarcity, MRS: mineral resource scarcity, HT: human toxicity, TE: terrestrial ecotoxicity, FE: freshwater eutrophication, TA: terrestrial acidification, ME: marine eutrophication).

in an increased amount of mineral phosphate fertilizer that can be substituted in S2 -0.09 kg CO2 eq) and S3 -0.08 kg CO2 eq) compared to S1 0.06 kg CO2 eq). Regarding the substitution of mineral N fertilizer, the direct emissions of NH3 during post treatment in S3, result in less am- monium nitrate substituted in S3 -0.54 CO2 eq), compared to S1 ( 0.78 kg CO2 eq) and S2 (-0.73 kg CO2 eq). The resource losses through the NH3 emissions are the determining factor for the lower environmental performance of S3, compared to the other two scenarios. Substitution of other types of mineral fertilizer could potentially result in other environ- mental footprints.

The experimental setup for the digestate spreading provides insights on the emissions of different digestate products. The post-treatment scenarios S2 and S3 perform better than S1 due to reduced loss of resources in terms of air emission after spreading. More precisely, the digestate spreading ac- tivities in S1, S2, and S3, emit 1.37, 0.84, and 0.81 g N2O per FU, allowing for higher nutrient cycling in the digestate products. A similar pattern be- tween the scenarios is observed for NH3 emissions during spreading. Rehl and Müller (2011) calculated a global warming potential of 0.06 kg CO2 eq for solar drying and 0.1 kg CO2 eq for belt drying. Styles et al. (2018) cal- culated the environmental performance of biofertlizer production and the results show that from the best to worse practices, the global warming po- tential ranges from 0.22 to 0.41 kg CO2 eq. Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2015) cal- culated a global warming potential of 0.06-0.14 kg CO2 eq per FU. Previous studies present a higher global warming potential mostly related to emis- sions during the storage phase of the digestate. In the current study and the experimental set-up, the different types of digestate were applied di- rectly on soil, thus, not accounting for direct emissions during storage. Emissions during storage are CH4 emissions from the storage unit and spreading due to anaerobe conditions and N2O emissions from the storage and digestate application (Rehl and Müller, 2011). Higher storage emis- sions are measured in the conventional management of digestate, when it is stored for a long period of time, while they are radically reduced when the digestate is dried and phase separated. Emissions from digestate storage, in this study, could compromise the calculated avoided emissions, especially for S1, where the management of raw digestate is considered. The storage emissions during conventional digestate management, accord- ing to Rehl and Müller (2011), are approximately 0.05 kg CO2 eq per FU, which would reduce the avoided emissions in S1 to - 0.31 kg CO2 eq.

It worth noting that the direct emissions in S3 are lower than S1. The higher indirect emissions in S3, which define the final performance of the scenario, are generated from the production and consumption of chemicals and energy that are based on fossil resources. In a future scenario where fos- sil fuels are replaced by renewable energy, S3 could potentially perform better. A similar trend is noticed for the impact categories of terrestrial acid- ification, marine eutrophication, and terrestrial ecotoxicity.

Emission reduction may be obtained through optimization of the method applied for spreading, as it influences the amount of NH3 emissions to air significantly. As assessed by Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2015), injection of digestate into the soil can lead to lower emissions to air, while lower odor
emissions can also be achieved with the same method (Orzi et al., 2018; Zilio et al., 2021). Such spreading method would require increased fuel consumption and, thus, needs further assessment.

In terms of treatment, a comparison of a scenario where the drying step (included in S3) is replaced by composting would also be interesting (Grigatti et al., 2020). Composting would increase the NH3 losses through air emissions but would avoid the energy consumption for the drying process.

Increasing the substitution of mineral fertilizer products with the use of organic fertilizer has a great potential for increased environmental impact mitigation but it is crucial to monitor increased recycling through avoided air emission losses. The LCIA only considered the digestate as providing fer- tilizing value, while it also brings back organic matter to soil, providing car- bon sequestration services. The environmental benefit of carbon stock increase in the soil was not included in the present study. An additional mit- igation of global warming may be calculated assuming that 10% of the car- bon remains in the soil at a time horizon of 100 year (Seghetta et al., 2016a), resulting in a reduction in the net CO2 emission. This reduction cor- responds to 0.157, 0.136, and 0.145 kg CO2 eq for S1, S2 and S3, respec- tively. Such ecosystem services should be addressed in the future methodological development of LCIA and policy, as also suggested by Seghetta et al. (2016b).
35: Emissions related to each scenario (IC: impact category, S: scenario, GW: global warming, FRS: fossil resource scarcity, MRS: mineral resource scarcity, HT: human toxicity, TE: terrestrial ecotoxicity, FE: freshwater eutrophication, TA: terrestrial acidification, ME: marine eutrophication).

in an increased amount of mineral phosphate fertilizer that can be substituted in S2 -0.09 kg CO2 eq) and S3 -0.08 kg CO2 eq) compared to S1 0.06 kg CO2 eq). Regarding the substitution of mineral N fertilizer, the direct emissions of NH3 during post treatment in S3, result in less am- monium nitrate substituted in S3 -0.54 CO2 eq), compared to S1 ( 0.78 kg CO2 eq) and S2 (-0.73 kg CO2 eq). The resource losses through the NH3 emissions are the determining factor for the lower environmental performance of S3, compared to the other two scenarios. Substitution of other types of mineral fertilizer could potentially result in other environ- mental footprints.

The experimental setup for the digestate spreading provides insights on the emissions of different digestate products. The post-treatment scenarios S2 and S3 perform better than S1 due to reduced loss of resources in terms of air emission after spreading. More precisely, the digestate spreading ac- tivities in S1, S2, and S3, emit 1.37, 0.84, and 0.81 g N2O per FU, allowing for higher nutrient cycling in the digestate products. A similar pattern be- tween the scenarios is observed for NH3 emissions during spreading. Rehl and Müller (2011) calculated a global warming potential of 0.06 kg CO2 eq for solar drying and 0.1 kg CO2 eq for belt drying. Styles et al. (2018) cal- culated the environmental performance of biofertlizer production and the results show that from the best to worse practices, the global warming po- tential ranges from 0.22 to 0.41 kg CO2 eq. Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2015) cal- culated a global warming potential of 0.06-0.14 kg CO2 eq per FU. Previous studies present a higher global warming potential mostly related to emis- sions during the storage phase of the digestate. In the current study and the experimental set-up, the different types of digestate were applied di- rectly on soil, thus, not accounting for direct emissions during storage. Emissions during storage are CH4 emissions from the storage unit and spreading due to anaerobe conditions and N2O emissions from the storage and digestate application (Rehl and Müller, 2011). Higher storage emis- sions are measured in the conventional management of digestate, when it is stored for a long period of time, while they are radically reduced when the digestate is dried and phase separated. Emissions from digestate storage, in this study, could compromise the calculated avoided emissions, especially for S1, where the management of raw digestate is considered. The storage emissions during conventional digestate management, accord- ing to Rehl and Müller (2011), are approximately 0.05 kg CO2 eq per FU, which would reduce the avoided emissions in S1 to - 0.31 kg CO2 eq.

It worth noting that the direct emissions in S3 are lower than S1. The higher indirect emissions in S3, which define the final performance of the scenario, are generated from the production and consumption of chemicals and energy that are based on fossil resources. In a future scenario where fos- sil fuels are replaced by renewable energy, S3 could potentially perform better. A similar trend is noticed for the impact categories of terrestrial acid- ification, marine eutrophication, and terrestrial ecotoxicity.
