The post-treatment scenarios S2 and S3 perform better than S1 due to reduced loss of resources in terms of air emission after spreading. More precisely, the digestate spreading ac- tivities in S1, S2, and S3, emit 1.37, 0.84, and 0.81 g N2O per FU, allowing for higher nutrient cycling in the digestate products. A similar pattern be- tween the scenarios is observed for NH3 emissions during spreading. Rehl and Müller (2011) calculated a global warming potential of 0.06 kg CO2 eq for solar drying and 0.1 kg CO2 eq for belt drying. Styles et al. (2018) cal- culated the environmental performance of biofertlizer production and the results show that from the best to worse practices, the global warming po- tential ranges from 0.22 to 0.41 kg CO2 eq. Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2015) cal- culated a global warming potential of 0.06-0.14 kg CO2 eq per FU. Previous studies present a higher global warming potential mostly related to emis- sions during the storage phase of the digestate. In the current study and the experimental set-up, the different types of digestate were applied di- rectly on soil, thus, not accounting for direct emissions during storage. Emissions during storage are CH4 emissions from the storage unit and spreading due to anaerobe conditions and N2O emissions from the storage and digestate application (Rehl and Müller, 2011). Higher storage emis- sions are measured in the conventional management of digestate, when it is stored for a long period of time, while they are radically reduced when the digestate is dried and phase separated. Emissions from digestate storage, in this study, could compromise the calculated avoided emissions, especially for S1, where the management of raw digestate is considered. The storage emissions during conventional digestate management, accord- ing to Rehl and Müller (2011), are approximately 0.05 kg CO2 eq per FU, which would reduce the avoided emissions in S1 to - 0.31 kg CO2 eq.

It worth noting that the direct emissions in S3 are lower than S1. The higher indirect emissions in S3, which define the final performance of the scenario, are generated from the production and consumption of chemicals and energy that are based on fossil resources. In a future scenario where fos- sil fuels are replaced by renewable energy, S3 could potentially perform better. A similar trend is noticed for the impact categories of terrestrial acid- ification, marine eutrophication, and terrestrial ecotoxicity.

Emission reduction may be obtained through optimization of the method applied for spreading, as it influences the amount of NH3 emissions to air significantly. As assessed by Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2015), injection of digestate into the soil can lead to lower emissions to air, while lower odor
emissions can also be achieved with the same method (Orzi et al., 2018; Zilio et al., 2021). Such spreading method would require increased fuel consumption and, thus, needs further assessment.

In terms of treatment, a comparison of a scenario where the drying step (included in S3) is replaced by composting would also be interesting (Grigatti et al., 2020). Composting would increase the NH3 losses through air emissions but would avoid the energy consumption for the drying process.

Increasing the substitution of mineral fertilizer products with the use of organic fertilizer has a great potential for increased environmental impact mitigation but it is crucial to monitor increased recycling through avoided air emission losses. The LCIA only considered the digestate as providing fer- tilizing value, while it also brings back organic matter to soil, providing car- bon sequestration services. The environmental benefit of carbon stock increase in the soil was not included in the present study. An additional mit- igation of global warming may be calculated assuming that 10% of the car- bon remains in the soil at a time horizon of 100 year (Seghetta et al., 2016a), resulting in a reduction in the net CO2 emission. This reduction cor- responds to 0.157, 0.136, and 0.145 kg CO2 eq for S1, S2 and S3, respec- tively. Such ecosystem services should be addressed in the future methodological development of LCIA and policy, as also suggested by Seghetta et al. (2016b).
42: The substitution of mineral fertilizer has a positive effect on the reduc- tion of fossil-based material consumption. For S1 (-0.053 kg oil eq) and S2 0.049 kg oil eq), this results in a net negative fossil resource scarcity value, which means that the fossil resource depletion is reduced. On the other hand, S3 (0.002 kg oil eq) represents a more energy intensive post- treatment technology, and this results in a net positive value and depletion of fossil resources. The latter is the result of the fossil-based materials con- sumed for the post-treatment process, such as electricity and sulfuric acid production. For the impact category of mineral resource scarcity, S1 kg Cu eq) and S2 -5.1.10-3 kg Cu eq), perform better than S3 (-3.5.10- kg Cu eq) due to the higher electricity consumption, the use of sulfuric acid, and the lower substitution of ammonium nitrate caused by the N losses in the post-treatment activities. Similar numeric re- sults are calculated for aggregated abiotic resource depletion (fossil and mineral) from Styles et al. (2018) that range from - 0.001 to 0.003 kg Sb eq. Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2015) calculated a fossil resource depletion equal to 0.01-0.02 kg oil eq.

Only fossil depletion impacts should be temporarily allowed to become worse upon advancement of the post-treatment processes because of an in- creased energy need within the post-treatment. However, this increase is dependent on the energy mix of the country where the post-treatment OC- curs. The impacts from fossil energy consumption will be reduced upon a gradual transformation into renewable energy supply. The current study did not consider the use of the energy from the AD plant as an input for
the post-treatment steps, which would have improved the environmental results, as shown by Rehl and Müller (2011) who compared different sources of energy inputs.

44: The impact categories human toxicity and terrestrial ecotoxicity show a similar pattern for all three scenarios. There is a trend for less avoided im- pacts in S3 0.58 kg 1,4-DCB eq) that is associated with the higher elec- tricity demand (0.38 kg 1,4-DCB eq) and the production of sulfuric acid (0.31 kg 1,4-DCB) in the terrestrial ecotoxicity category. For the human non-carcinogenic toxicity, the environmental impacts derive almost exclu- sively (98-99%) from the emissions of the digestate spreading. The envi- ronmental performance for the human non-carcinogenic toxicity for S1, S2, and S3 is 28.8, 33, and 33.3 kg 1,4-DCB eq while for the impact category of terrestrial ecotoxicity, S1, S2, and S3, have an impact of 1.58, 1.57, -0.58 kg 1,4-DCB eq, respectively. S3 performs the lowest in both impact categories. Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2015) found a lower environmental im- pact of 2.46.10 kg 1,4-DCB eq for terrestrial ecotoxicity and 0.003 kg 1,4-DCB eq for human toxicity.

The partitioning of the hazardous substances prefers the solid fraction of the digestate (Appendix A4). An in-depth analysis of the processes contrib- uting to the negative environmental impact on human toxicity showed that zinc and lead emissions to soil are the most relevant, while for terrestrial ecotoxicity, the higher contributors to the environmental impact are silver and mercury to air from the electricity consumption.

For the impact category of human toxicity, the direct emissions are re- sponsible for the negative environmental impact, while for the terrestrial ecotoxicity, the direct emissions have a negligible contribution on the total impact. Thus, S1 performs the best for the human toxicity due to less direct emissions that are reflected, however, in worse performance in the global warming impact category. Similarly, S1 and S2 perform better in the terrestrial ecotoxicity category due to the higher substituted mineral fertilizer amount and the lower energy and material consumption com- pared to S3. Environmental and human health impacts should not increase upon closing the biological nutrient cycle.

According to the present regulatory standards (Appendix A5 and A7), the organic fertilizer presents a good product quality as its content in heavy metals is lower than the thresholds for organic soil improvers in France, except for zinc and cadmium in the liquid digestate in the S2. Excluding the liquid digestate, the concentration of cadmium in the post-treated digestate in this study is comparable to the Danish un- treated manure and lower or comparable to the content in Danish sludge.

The concentration level of heavy metals in the organic fertilizer is below the limits adopted in the European fertilizer Regulation (European Commis- sion, 2019) as well as below the quality criteria of the Danish Sludge Direc- tive (Danish Ministry of Environment, 2018). Still, according to a draft proposal on the European regulation of cadmium in fertilizers (Chemicals Unit of DG Enterprise, 2003) a concentration level of 136 mg Cd-kg- (60 mg Cd-kg P2O5) will lead to cadmium accumulation in agricultural soils. A concentration level of 45 mg Cd-kg P is considered an acceptable value, even though such product standard would still lead to accumulation in soil (Dam Jensen et al., 2015; Pizzol et al., 2014).

As presented in the study of Pizzol et al. (2014), it is possible to mone- tize human health cost of cadmium emission to soil as a consequence of the presence of micro-pollutants in the individual fertilizer products. Both the efficiency in recycling of the nutrient content in digestate, but also the fertilizing value, and the concentration of micro-pollutants influence the environmental performance of the technologies. This implies the impor- tance of developing science-based policy regulations for the micro- pollutant content per mass unit of plant available P. Like N, the application of organic P fertilizer on agricultural soil needs to be included in the na- tional regulations. This will allow the moderation of the mineral fertilizers' use, especially when it can be adjusted to the degree of self-supply and demand.

48: The net freshwater eutrophication potential is calculated close to zero due to the balanced impacts of the substitution of mineral by organic fertil- izers and the eutrophication effect caused by the spreading of digestate. The substitution includes the production and use of mineral fertilizer, which ex- plains the capacity to counter-balance the negative impact of the emissions after spreading. Negative values are obtained for all scenarios, with the total impact score for S1, S2, and S3 being -7.9.10-5, 9.0.10-5 and 3.10 kg P eq, respectively. Direct emissions from the digestate ap- plication and avoided mineral fertilizer are lower in S1, due to the higher availability of citrate soluble P in the post-treated digestate. The highest contributing process is the spreading of digestate on agricultural land, which for S1, S2, and S3 was calculated to 1.2.10-3 1.7.10- and 1.6.10 kg P eq, respectively.

The net marine eutrophication potential for S1, S2, and S3 is 3.1.10- 2.7.10- and 1.1.101 kg N eq per FU, with a significant share of the con- tribution (<99%) to be from the digestate spreading activities. The eutro- phication effect in the marine environment is due to the runoff of N and N compounds to soil and their transport to the marine system, with N being the limiting nutrient. The contribution of other processes to the ma- rine eutrophication is negligible.

50: For terrestrial acidification, a better environmental performance is ob- served for S2 (0.11 kg SO2 eq) and S3 (0.09 kg SO2 eq) compared to S1 (0.18 kg SO2 eq). Among the substances listed in the LCI, only the NH3 emission has a characterization factor that describes its effect on terrestrial acidification. Direct emissions are the most relevant contributors to the total impact in this category. Lower NH3 emissions during spreading of the digestate in S2 and S3 explain the better performance of the post- treatment scenarios in this impact category. S3 also considers NH3 emis- sions during the drying post-treatment. High NH3 emissions are reported in literature during the digestate storage, which could contribute to the acidification potential by 0.007 kg SO2 eq (Rehl and Müller, 2011), further increasing the environmental burden in this impact category. In a more ad- vanced scenario, NH3 volatilized during drying would be trapped in acid and valorized which would still decrease the terrestrial acidification poten- tial of S3. Styles et al. (2018) resulted in a similar terrestrial acidification potential of 0.008 kg SO2 eq.
51: <header>3.6. Sensitivity analysis </header>

A sensitivity analysis was performed to evaluate the effect of the fertil- izer substitution methodology and substitution factors used in the study. In the main scenarios only the citrate soluble P and 40% of the total N con- tent were considered for substitution. In the sensitivity analysis the total content of N and P were considered to substitute ammonium nitrate and phosphate fertilizers, respectively. The results are presented in Fig. 3. The order of the three scenarios in respect to their environmental per- formance is the same for all the impact categories, except freshwater eutro- phication and mineral resource scarcity. The higher amount of substituted mineral fertilizer and its avoided direct emissions results in numerically higher avoided impacts for all impact categories. In the mineral resource scarcity impact category, for the sensitivity analysis, S1 -0.009 kg Cu eq) performs better than S2 (-0.0088 kg Cu eq) and S3 0.006 kg Cu eq). Similar for the freshwater eutrophication S1, S2, and S3 has a total im- pact of 1.59*10-4, and 8.1.10- kg P eq, respectively. The quantified environmental impacts associated with the energy and material consumption remain numerically the same in the main scenarios and the -respective to each scenario- sensitivity analysis, since no changes in the amount of energy and materials were considered. The impacts associ- ated with the process emissions and the substituted products influence the results of the sensitivity analysis. In the freshwater eutrophication, the spreading of digestate and the substituted products have different numerical

The direct impacts from the digestate spreading also increase in the sensitivity analysis; more for S1 (0.0018 kg P eq), followed by S2 (0.002 kg P eq) and S3 (0.0018 kg P eq). However, this increase does not compensate for the avoided impacts from fertilizer substitution.

The changes in the impact category of mineral resource scarcity follows the same trend, however, it is only reflected in the avoided environmental impact of the substituted products. The larger substituted fraction in S1 in the sensitivity analysis result in S1 having the greatest avoided impact po- tential -0.009 kg Cu eq), followed by S2 0.0088 kg Cu eq) and S3 0.0061 kg Cu eq).

A fourth scenario was also assessed, reflecting the difference between stationary energy use for digestate drying, modelled in S3, and longer trans- portation distances in S4. The results of the sensitivity analysis are provided in Table 2.

Table 2 shows that for global warming, freshwater eutrophication, human toxicity, terrestrial ecotoxicity, and mineral resource scarcity S4 per- forms better than S3. This implies that the emissions from the drying phase are more than the increased emission associated to the transportation of wet digestate. Thus, in the case where spreading is not feasible locally (e.g. ex- cess nutrients zone), a drying post-treatment demonstrates to be a relevant option for solid digestate valorization, however, there are environmental impacts associated with specific impact categories that need to be consid- ered. According to the regulations, the transport distance of the liquid con- centrated products as UF retentate and RO concentrate is restricted to less than 20 km and has, therefore, been omitted from the sensitivity analysis.

The direct impacts from the digestate spreading also increase in the sensitivity analysis; more for S1 (0.0018 kg P eq), followed by S2 (0.002 kg P eq) and S3 (0.0018 kg P eq). A fourth scenario was also assessed, reflecting the difference between stationary energy use for digestate drying, modelled in S3, and longer trans- portation distances in S4. The results of the sensitivity analysis are provided in Table 2.

Table 2 shows that for global warming, freshwater eutrophication, human toxicity, terrestrial ecotoxicity, and mineral resource scarcity S4 per- forms better than S3. This implies that the emissions from the drying phase are more than the increased emission associated to the transportation of wet digestate. Thus, in the case where spreading is not feasible locally (e.g. ex- cess nutrients zone), a drying post-treatment demonstrates to be a relevant option for solid digestate valorization, however, there are environmental impacts associated with specific impact categories that need to be consid- ered. According to the regulations, the transport distance of the liquid con- centrated products as UF retentate and RO concentrate is restricted to less than 20 km and has, therefore, been omitted from the sensitivity analysis.
57: Measurements of the post-treatment process-based emission are per- formed at lab-scale and may differ from full-scale measurements. Data and model uncertainty should be considered when interpreting the LCIA results.

The gaseous emissions from spreading of the fertilizer products are mea- sured at laboratory scale. Such emissions during spreading are the main contributor to global warming and terrestrial acidification and should be verified at real scale to lower the uncertainty in LCIA results. Furthermore, as for the carbon sequestration in soil, there seem to be a need for regulatory instruments acknowledging the implementation of emission reduction tech- nologies within the agricultural and circular bioeconomy sector in general.

In this case study, some steps of the digestate post-treatment were omit- ted, such as the storage step before spreading. These storage steps could lead to air emissions, such as NH3 and N20. However, it could potentially decrease some emissions after spreading, which were measured for this study on non-stored products. Long digestate storage periods can increase the total emissions, especially when raw digestate is stored, without being post-treated (Rehl and Müller, 2011).

On a local scale, the spreading of the digestate may become restricted due to regulations or simply because the local supply exceeds the demand from local farmers (and/or the nutrient demand of the local cropland) (Damgaard Poulsen et al., 2019). One of the interests in performing ad- vanced post-treatment of digestate is the ability to export organic soil improvers or fertilizers to markets outside the local areas. Further post- treatment of the dried solid digestate (S3), such as pelletizing, could produce other final products.

In a future low-carbon/renewable energy scenario, the total CO2 foot- print would equal the process CO2 footprint implying no energy-related emissions from the post-treatment. Considering pelletizing of dried digestate, it would have increased the energy consumption through the post-treatment scenario, but it might have decreased emissions after spread- ing (Alemi et al., 2010), as for composted products (Laville et al., 2014). It must be yet noted that S3 is based on a drying up to 80% DM. Pelletizing could be performed on less dried digestate with lower energy consumption.

Considering the diverse situations that may exist, it would be interesting to assess scenarios where a fraction of the raw digestate is directly applied on soil (up to the spreading area limitations) and another fraction is upgraded through highly performant technologies for export and market valorization.

60: The study assessed three digestate utilization scenarios. Scenario 1 describes a system where the raw digestate is directly applied on soil. In sce- nario 2, the raw digestate is processed by phase separation (centrifugation) with two output phases (liquid and solid digestate) that are spread on agri- cultural soil. Scenario 3 describes a more advanced post-treatment system, where the raw digestate is phase separated by centrifugation followed by drying of the solid digestate and further processing of the liquid digestate with a membrane filtration unit and a reverse osmosis unit. Among the studied scenarios, in scenario 3 it was possible to obtain three different types of products that have fertilizing value. These and other high-value products might answer diverse agronomic needs and sufficient concentra- tion flexibility for substituting synthetic fertilizers products. The digestate post-treatment is a sustainable solution able to tackle the problem of excess nutrients and their management in agricultural areas. It could replace con- ventional nitrogen removal processes (aerobic biological treatment) keep- ing more nutrients in the digestate products but minimizing the losses through air and soil emissions.

The life cycle assessment showed that for the impact categories of global warming, freshwater eutrophication, and mineral resource scarcity sce- nario 2 was evaluated to have the lowest environmental impact. For human non-carcinogenic toxicity, terrestrial ecotoxicity, and fossil resource scarcity scenario 1 has the lowest emissions and for terrestrial acidification and marine eutrophication scenario 3 was calculated as the most efficient. The environmental performance of the system could be further improved by including the contribution to climate change mitigation potential from an increased soil carbon stock. Methodological development to include such ecosystem services is ongoing and represents a central service deliv- ered from circular management of organic residues.
